From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Female domestic worker

For other uses, see [Maid (disambiguation)](/wiki/Maid_(disambiguation) "Maid (disambiguation)").

"Chambermaid" redirects here. For other uses, see [The Chambermaid](/wiki/The_Chambermaid_(disambiguation) "The Chambermaid (disambiguation)").

"Housemaid" redirects here. For other uses, see [The Housemaid](/wiki/The_Housemaid_(disambiguation) "The Housemaid (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Globe icon. | The examples and perspective in this article **deal primarily with [the English-speaking world](/wiki/English-speaking_world "English-speaking world") and do not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Maid "Talk:Maid"), or [create a new article](/wiki/Wikipedia:Article_wizard "Wikipedia:Article wizard"), as appropriate. *(June 2015)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Smedley_maid_illustration_1906.jpg/250px-Smedley_maid_illustration_1906.jpg)](/wiki/File:Smedley_maid_illustration_1906.jpg)

Illustration by [William Thomas Smedley](/wiki/William_Thomas_Smedley "William Thomas Smedley"), 1906

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Raimundo_Madrazo_-_La_Toilette.jpg/250px-Raimundo_Madrazo_-_La_Toilette.jpg)](/wiki/File:Raimundo_Madrazo_-_La_Toilette.jpg)

*La Toilette* by [Raimundo de Madrazo y Garreta](/wiki/Raimundo_Madrazo "Raimundo Madrazo"), c. 1890 – c. 1900

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/ModelC5_1912.jpg/250px-ModelC5_1912.jpg)](/wiki/File:ModelC5_1912.jpg)

A maid cleaning in [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") in 1912.

A **maid**, **housemaid**, or **maidservant** is a female [domestic worker](/wiki/Domestic_worker "Domestic worker"). In the [Victorian era](/wiki/Victorian_era "Victorian era"), domestic service was the second-largest category of employment in England and Wales, after agricultural work.[[1]](#cite_note-1) In developed Western nations, full-time maids are now typically only found in the wealthiest households. In other parts of the world (mainly within the continent of Asia), maids remain common in urban middle-class households.

*Maid* in [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") meant an unmarried woman, especially a young one, or specifically a [virgin](/wiki/Virgin "Virgin"). These meanings lived on in English until recent times (and are still familiar from literature and folk music), alongside the sense of the word as a type of servant.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

Maids perform typical domestic chores such as [laundry](/wiki/Laundry "Laundry"), [ironing](/wiki/Ironing "Ironing"), [cleaning](/wiki/Cleaning "Cleaning") the house, grocery shopping, [cooking](/wiki/Cooking "Cooking"), and caring for household pets. They may also [take care of children](/wiki/Child_care "Child care"), although there are more specific occupations for this, such as [nanny](/wiki/Nanny "Nanny"). In some poor countries, maids take care of the elderly and people with disabilities. Many maids are required by their employers to wear a [uniform](/wiki/Uniform "Uniform").

In the contemporary [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), comparatively few households can afford live-in domestic help, usually relying on [cleaners](/wiki/Cleaner "Cleaner"), employed directly or through an agency ([maid service](/wiki/Maid_service "Maid service")).
Many services historically provided by maids have been [substituted](/wiki/Substitute_good "Substitute good") with [home appliances](/wiki/Home_appliances "Home appliances").

In less developed nations, various factors ensure a labour source for domestic work: very large differences in the income of urban and rural households, widespread poverty, fewer [educated women](/wiki/Female_education "Female education"), and limited opportunities for the employment of less educated women.

Legislation in many countries makes certain living conditions, working hours, or minimum wage a requirement of domestic service. Nonetheless, the work of a maid has always been hard, involving a full day, and extensive duties. Maids would be familiar with hard work and typically worked long hours in a week.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## By location

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: By location")]

### Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Europe")]

#### British Isles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: British Isles")]

Maids were once part of an elaborate hierarchy in [great houses](/wiki/Great_house "Great house"), where the [retinue](/wiki/Retinue "Retinue") of servants stretched up to the housekeeper and butler, responsible for female and male employees respectively. It was the most common way that women could earn money, especially lower class women.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) Domestic workers, particularly those low in the hierarchy, such as maids and [footmen](/wiki/Footmen "Footmen"), were expected to remain unmarried while in service.[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) They sometimes had their own section of rooms in the house, though they were often far away from the other rooms and more simply furnished than the rest of the house.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In Victorian England, all middle-class families would have employed staff, but for most small households, this would be only one employee, the maid of all work. Some households employed maids-of-all-work as young as twelve in 19th-century England. They often worked from five in the morning until late in the evening on a wage of £6 to £9 per year.[[9]](#cite_note-9) They typically only had one or two days off in a month.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Historically, many maids suffered from [prepatellar bursitis](/wiki/Prepatellar_bursitis "Prepatellar bursitis"), an inflammation of the [prepatellar bursa](/wiki/Prepatellar_bursa "Prepatellar bursa") caused by long periods spent on the knees for purposes of scrubbing and fire-lighting, leading to the condition attracting the colloquial name of "housemaid's knee".[[11]](#cite_note-11)

As the end of the 19th century neared, the relationship between employer and servant changed.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By the end of the nineteenth century, there was a decline in the demand for domestic staff entirely.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

#### Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Types")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Joshua_Reynolds_-_Tysoe_Hancock_and_his_Family_with_an_Indian_Maid_-_WGA19338.jpg/250px-Joshua_Reynolds_-_Tysoe_Hancock_and_his_Family_with_an_Indian_Maid_-_WGA19338.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joshua_Reynolds_-_Tysoe_Hancock_and_his_Family_with_an_Indian_Maid_-_WGA19338.jpg)

[George Clive](/wiki/George_Clive_(died_1779) "George Clive (died 1779)") and his family with an Indian maid, painted 1765. As she appears to be caring for the child, she may be an *[aya](/wiki/Amah_(occupation) "Amah (occupation)")*.

Maids traditionally have a fixed position in the hierarchy of the large households, and although there is overlap between definitions (dependent on the size of the household), the positions themselves would typically be rigidly adhered to. The usual classifications of maid in a large household are:

* [Lady's maid](/wiki/Lady%27s_maid "Lady's maid"): a senior servant who reported directly to the [lady](/wiki/Lady "Lady") of the house, but ranked beneath the [housekeeper](/wiki/Housekeeper_(domestic_worker) "Housekeeper (domestic worker)"), and accompanied her lady on travel. She took care of her mistress's clothes and hair, and sometimes served as confidante.
* House-maid: a generic term for maids whose function was chiefly "above stairs", and were usually a little older, and better paid. Where a household included multiple housemaids, the roles were often subdivided as below.
  + Head house-maid: the senior house maid, reporting to the housekeeper. (Also called "house parlour maid" in an establishment with only one or two upstairs maids).
  + Parlour maid: cleaned and tidied reception rooms and living areas by morning, and often served refreshments at [afternoon tea](/wiki/Afternoon_tea "Afternoon tea"), and sometimes also dinner. They tidied studies and libraries, and (with footmen) answered bells calling for service.[[13]](#cite_note-13)
  + Chamber maid: cleaned and maintained the bedrooms, ensured fires were lit in fireplaces, and supplied hot water.
  + Laundry maid: maintained bedding and towels. They also washed, dried, and ironed clothes for the whole household, including the servants.
  + Under house parlour maid: performed a parlour maid's duties below stairs.
* [Nursery maid](/wiki/Nursemaid "Nursemaid"): also an "upstairs maid", but one who worked in the children's nursery, maintaining fires, cleanliness, and good order. Reported to the nanny rather than the housekeeper. The nursemaid would often stay with one family for years or as long as their services were needed.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* [Kitchen maid](/wiki/Kitchen_maid_(domestic_worker) "Kitchen maid (domestic worker)"): a "below stairs" maid who reported to the cook, and assisted in running the kitchens.
  + Head kitchen maid: where multiple kitchen maids were employed, the "head kitchen maid" was effectively a [deputy to the cook](/wiki/Line_cook "Line cook"), engaged largely in the plainer and simpler cooking (sometimes cooking the servants' meals).
  + Under kitchen maid: where multiple kitchen maids were employed, these were the staff who prepared vegetables, peeled potatoes, and assisted in presentation of finished cooking for serving.
* [Scullery maid](/wiki/Scullery_maid "Scullery maid"): the lowest grade of "below stairs" maid, reporting to the cook, the scullery maids were responsible for washing cutlery, crockery, and glassware, and scrubbing kitchen floors, as well as monitoring ovens while kitchen maids ate their own supper.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* [Between maid](/wiki/Between_maid "Between maid"), sometimes known as a "tweeny": roughly equivalent in status to scullery maids, and often paid less, between maids in a large household waited on the senior servants ([butler](/wiki/Butler "Butler"), housekeeper, and [cook](/wiki/Cook_(domestic_worker) "Cook (domestic worker)")) and were therefore answerable to all three department heads, often leading to friction in their employment.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
* [Still room maid](/wiki/Still_room_maid "Still room maid"): a junior maid employed in the [still room](/wiki/Still_room "Still room"); as the work involved the supply of alcohol, cosmetics, medicines, and cooking ingredients across all departments of the house, the still room maids were part of the "between staff", jointly answerable to all three department heads.

In more modest households, a single **maid-of-all-work** or **skivvy** was often the only staff. It is possible this word originates from the Italian for slave ("*schiavo*"—"owned person").

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Asia")]

See also: [Foreign domestic helpers in Hong Kong](/wiki/Foreign_domestic_helpers_in_Hong_Kong "Foreign domestic helpers in Hong Kong")

An *ayi* (*aunt* in Mandarin) works as a domestic helper in China, and occasionally provides personalized childcare.

### Middle East

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Middle East")]

See also: [Migrant workers in the Gulf region](/wiki/Migrant_workers_in_the_Gulf_region "Migrant workers in the Gulf region"), [History of slavery in the Muslim world](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_the_Muslim_world "History of slavery in the Muslim world"), [House slave](/wiki/House_slave "House slave"), [Kafala system](/wiki/Kafala_system "Kafala system"), and [Foreign workers in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Foreign_workers_in_Saudi_Arabia "Foreign workers in Saudi Arabia")

In the [history of slavery in the Muslim world](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_the_Muslim_world "History of slavery in the Muslim world"), domestic servitude was one of the driving forces behind the slave trade.[[17]](#cite_note-17)
The ideal of [Islamic sex segregation](/wiki/Islam_and_gender_segregation "Islam and gender segregation") made it difficult for free Muslim women to work as servants, at the same time, the sex segregation made it difficult for male servants to access the harem women's quarter of the house, resulting in difficulty in Muslim women to have access to domestic servants.
This dilemma resulted in the Muslim world using [house slaves](/wiki/House_slave "House slave") for domestic servants rather than free servants. By [Islamic law](/wiki/Islamic_law "Islamic law"), slaves owned by Muslims were to be foreign non-Muslims ([kafirs](/wiki/Kafir "Kafir")) who were by definition legitimate targets for enslavement, since the Muslim world of [dar al-islam](/wiki/Dar_al-islam "Dar al-islam") was by definition at war with the non-Muslim world of [dar al-harb](/wiki/Dar_al-harb "Dar al-harb") ("House of War").[[18]](#cite_note-18)
Female slaves were thus not subjected to the same Islamic customs. It also made it possible for male slaves to be made eunuchs. This solved both the dilemma of female servants being able to work in the household of a family as well as for a male servant to enter the women's quarter of a home.
The slave trade to the Muslim world prioritized women and children, though men were also trafficked.[[19]](#cite_note-Freamon,_B._K._2019_p._285-19)
In bigger households there were typically a mix of male and female house slaves.[[20]](#cite_note-Islamic_World_2018_p.144-20)

In the Islamic world, female slaves were normally purchased for one of two reasons: for the purpose of [sexual slavery](/wiki/Sexual_slavery "Sexual slavery") as [concubines](/wiki/History_of_concubinage_in_the_Muslim_world "History of concubinage in the Muslim world") or as domestic servants, and used for service in the [harems](/wiki/Harem "Harem").[[20]](#cite_note-Islamic_World_2018_p.144-20)
Women domestic slaves were purchased to perform chores for the sex segregated women in the harem.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
Women domestic slaves were used for all sorts of household chores normally performed by women, and could also be sent outside of the harem to perform tasks for the harem women in the city outside of the home.[[20]](#cite_note-Islamic_World_2018_p.144-20)
Black African women were most commonly used for domestic slave labor in Egypt, the Arab world and the Ottoman Empire.
[[19]](#cite_note-Freamon,_B._K._2019_p._285-19)
In the Islamic Middle East, African women – trafficked via the [trans-Saharan slave trade](/wiki/Trans-Saharan_slave_trade "Trans-Saharan slave trade"), the [Red Sea slave trade](/wiki/Red_Sea_slave_trade "Red Sea slave trade") and the [Indian Ocean slave trade](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_slave_trade "Indian Ocean slave trade") – were primarily used as domestic house servants and not exclusively for sexual slavery, while white women, trafficked via the [Black Sea slave trade](/wiki/Black_Sea_slave_trade "Black Sea slave trade") and [Circassian slave trade](/wiki/Circassian_slave_trade "Circassian slave trade"), were preferred for the use of [concubines](/wiki/Concubinage_in_Islam "Concubinage in Islam") (sex slaves) or wives, and there was therefore a constant demand for them in the Middle East.[[22]](#cite_note-Gordon,_Murray_1989_p.79-89-22)
The women of the family monitored the work of the domestic slaves, decided what to do with the leftovers of food, and made sure that the slaves performed the Islamic prayers.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

Most of the Middle East were included in the Ottoman Empire. When the [slavery in the Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_Ottoman_Empire "Slavery in the Ottoman Empire") were nominally abolished by the [Kanunname of 1889](/wiki/Kanunname_of_1889 "Kanunname of 1889"), the former house slaves normally continued to work as nominally free maidservants; their employers could however now fire them instead of selling them on the slave market, creating a class of free servants. After the closure of the slave market [Avret Pazari](/wiki/Avret_Pazarlar%C4%B1 "Avret Pazarları") in Constantinople by the [Disestablishment of the Istanbul Slave Market](/wiki/Disestablishment_of_the_Istanbul_Slave_Market "Disestablishment of the Istanbul Slave Market"), the [Young Turks](/wiki/Young_Turks "Young Turks") founded the [Hizmetçi İdaresi](/wiki/Hizmet%C3%A7i_%C4%B0daresi "Hizmetçi İdaresi") as a Servant Institution to assist former female slaves to find employment as domestic workers and maidservants in order to escape prostitution to survive, though the institution is reported to have functioned, in practice, as a slave market.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

House slaves were used in the Muslim Middle East until the mid 20th-century. Despite the Ottoman anti slavery reforms introduced in the 19th-century, chattel slavery continued to exist in the former Ottoman provinces in the Middle East after the dissolution of the Ottoman Empire in 1917–1920: while [slavery in Egypt](/wiki/Slavery_in_Egypt "Slavery in Egypt") was phased out after the [ban of the slave trade](/wiki/Anglo-Egyptian_Slave_Trade_Convention "Anglo-Egyptian Slave Trade Convention") in 1877–1884, existing slaves were noted as late as 1931;[[25]](#cite_note-25) [slavery in Iraq](/wiki/Slavery_in_Iraq "Slavery in Iraq") was banned after British pressure in 1924;[[26]](#cite_note-26) [slavery in Jordan](/wiki/Slavery_in_Jordan "Slavery in Jordan") was ended by the British in 1929;[[27]](#cite_note-27) [slavery in Lebanon](/wiki/Slavery_in_Lebanon "Slavery in Lebanon") as well as [slavery in Syria](/wiki/Slavery_in_Syria "Slavery in Syria") was banned by the French in 1931;[[28]](#cite_note-28) [slavery in Palestine](/wiki/Slavery_in_Palestine "Slavery in Palestine") still existed under the guise of clientage in 1934;[[29]](#cite_note-29) [slavery in Libya](/wiki/Slavery_in_Libya "Slavery in Libya") still existed in 1930s;[[30]](#cite_note-30) and [slavery in Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Slavery_in_Saudi_Arabia "Slavery in Saudi Arabia") lasted until it was abolished after pressure from the US in 1962,[[31]](#cite_note-31) with [slavery in Yemen](/wiki/Slavery_in_Yemen "Slavery in Yemen") being banned between 1962 and 1967.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

After the abolition of slavery in the Middle East in the 20th-century, chattel slavery was succeeded by the [kafala system](/wiki/Kafala_system "Kafala system").
Today, foreign women are employed in [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia "Saudi Arabia"), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait "Kuwait"), [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar "Qatar"), [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon"), [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") and [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") in large numbers to work as maids or other roles of domestic service, and are often vulnerable to multiple forms of abuse.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-the_guardian1-34)[[35]](#cite_note-humanrightswatch1-35)

### Southern Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Southern Africa")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Maid "Special:EditPage/Maid") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(October 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In some areas in the region, the word "maid" is avoided and is often referred to as a "helper" instead. This is most likely due to the fact that it sounds like a racially derogatory term in [Afrikaans](/wiki/Afrikaans "Afrikaans").[[36]](#cite_note-36) Maids in South Africa were referred to as domestic servants and they included men, women, and children. They were subject to low wages, lack of a social life, unfavorable working conditions, and even unaccommodating work hours.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The Afrikaans word for a mite (small arachnid) has been used demeaningly to refer to women of colour. The English word for a friend, "mate", is also avoided for this reason.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maid&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Further information: [French maid](/wiki/French_maid "French maid")

*La Grève des bonnes*, a 1906 satire about French maids going on strike

One of the most in-depth and enduring representations of the lives of several types of maid was seen in the 1970s television drama *[Upstairs, Downstairs](/wiki/Upstairs,_Downstairs_(1971_TV_series) "Upstairs, Downstairs (1971 TV series)")*, set in [England](/wiki/England "England") between 1903 and 1936. The lives of maids were well represented in the *[Downton Abbey](/wiki/Downton_Abbey "Downton Abbey")* series, set in England between 1912 and 1926 and shown from 2010 onward.

The American television drama *[The Gilded Age](/wiki/The_Gilded_Age_(TV_series) "The Gilded Age (TV series)")*, set in the 1880s in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), depicts the lives of maids living and working in the great houses of the [era](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age").

The main characters in the [NAMIC Vision Award](/w/index.php?title=NAMIC_Vision_Award&action=edit&redlink=1 "NAMIC Vision Award (page does not exist)")-nominated television series *[Devious Maids](/wiki/Devious_Maids "Devious Maids")* are four housemaids.
